commentBody:
Katie Hoody,
I read your note. 37A? 8D!
Alternative clue for 52D: "Split personality?"
Regarding the mini…Intelligent DESIGN? Ugh. Even worse than ESP or URI Geller.

I was awfully close to losing my gold star streak today. Took me a while, but eventually a couple clues in the top left and top right opened the floodgates. 

Harder than usual, for sure.
This puzzle was a total delight.

I enjoyed this twisty puzzle.  The NE was not my finest work, because I had not my finest hour instead of NOTMYFINESTWORK, I guessed that Big Sandy was the oldest, not the WIDEST, track, and I was unfamiliar with BRENDA Howard.  Eventually seeing GREATLAKE helped me undo my mistakes.

For anyone who has never seen a SEADRAGON, they are mysterious and beautiful creatures.
Finally! Lots of wordplay and just the right amount of trivia. This puzzle was much more fun for me than recent trivia-heavy grids, with plenty of delightful AHA moments.
26 across is wrong— single fits; not plural.

BRIO twice in one week. Never heard that word in my life before Sunday. Not sure how I would have worked out the SW without it.

Went through two wrong definitions of anchor and incorrectly tried last (as in the anchor of a race) and moor/dock. Took me a minute after I finished the puzzle to understand that DESK meant a news anchor.

Had ArmCANNON before AIRCANNON. Was considering argoN before ONION.

Tough puzzle, but never felt like I was stuck at any point. Kept making slow progress until I solved it

Emus ate this, trying again, sorry if it posts again:

You don’t know how relieved I was to see Katie Hoody’s byline today. Not just because I love Katie Hoody puzzles—and I do—but because EVERYONE loves Katie Hoody, and after yesterday’s nasty-fest in the comments I’m hoping we can all get along today. LETSPARTY! 

I was off to the races in the NW—while I couldn’t fit in rootbeerSODA and teeshirtCANNON the second half of each was enough to get me going. (Thanks for the Father’s Day call out).

The bottom half fell into place more gradually, about average difficulty for me with the usual Hoody gems—especially SNOWBIRDS and POWERTOOL (OK, I actually parsed that one that way first but “Saw getting turned on is” is pretty cute nonetheless.) Finally the NE which gave me my expected Saturday teeth pulling. I thought I was so smart pulling LAKEHURON out of my α$$ immediately, and was on the right track but not quite. Also CHORE before CHUTE and OLDEST before WIDEST and ALARM before TIMER. Finally I could say IMONTOYOU and smile at NERDALERT.

Last comment. Have to ask JG who yesterday seemed to think that only men “use specific facts and names and clues/answers”—umm, CHO, WEEB, DORIS, CROAT, HONG, BRENDA, CAPRI, HENRY—most of which I didn’t know but was happy to learn about and didn’t detract from my solve—um, Katie, have you  had a sex change lately? The rest of you, did this meet your rigorous standard of trivia and proper names?

Thanks Katie, brilliant as always.

Anyone else have DEcK for [Anchor position]? Sorry Doris (not Doric) Burke! Took me way too long to flyspeck that one.

For me, the NW was largely unfilled for the longest time (with only a sad, lone ONION), before I took a chance to partially fill out …SODA for [Dad’s pop, perhaps]. I’m still amazed how fast things can fall into place with a tiny foothold.

The misdirects and wordplay made this for me. Thanks Katie!
9 (!) is way too many expressions in one puzzle.
If you Google "not my finest," "hour" is the word it suggests first, and that was my choice as well. Nice to know that both Google and I were stumped on that one! :-)

I REALLY wanted this one!

About 40 minutes in, I was pumped.  Picture "Bronn" from Game of Thrones back when it was good, in full fight fury.

My loins were so girded that no one has ever seen loins girdeder, and testosterone fluxed through my bloodstream like Niagara Falls, but going through a straw.

It was just...

(pause while warrior Bronn takes the dogs out to poop)

... a Do or Die puzzle.  A battle to the death.  A battle beyond the death to the pain!

Oh, to be 70 again!

I guessing this puzzle is going to go over about like yesterday's (which I did not get).
Good stuff!
I gotta tell you, the number of images that flashed through my mind when I read the clue [Saw getting turned on, say], kinda made me feel...a bit younger, saw.

First time that I’ve ever come to the comments for assistance. I had DORIc and DEcK. Deck made sense for an anchor so I didn’t catch that Doric would be an odd first name. I had gone through checking and re-checking a number of times.

I still loved this puzzle. It gave me exactly what I looked for even before getting stuck. Every section was a slog. Thank you so much.
Good one. Sorta makes up for yesterdays dud.

WHOA!! That was another toughie!  But unlike Friday when I couldn't complete it, today I present to you my huzzah and my hip hip-huramba!! 💃🕺 LETSPARTY!!  🕺💃

I would not impress anyone with my time, not that I ever share it, but this one was too much fun to stop and I really never thought I couldn't get it, like I did with yesterday's. 

Or it might be today's. Meaning Friday's. Is today Friday still?  If so, which year?

Anyhow, perhaps if timing matters, ITSNOTMYFINESTWORK, but by my own metrics of completion without any lookups, I'm pretty proud of it!

I'd be hard pressed to say a favorite clue but so many of them made me giggle with childish glee...   The clues for POWERTOOL, SNOWBIRDS, LETSPARTY, and PLOTPOINT would certainly be at the top of my list. 

Even if I did want "Get down" to be partay, BOOGIE also made me smile. 

Fun puzzle! Really enjoyed this one! 

@SP:  lacking neutrality this time? Har!

I'm relieved to know that small storage units aren't s•a•fES, and that allies don't share an ENEM•a•. Yeah, I know, NOT MY FINEST WORK... But I got it done in the end, so LET'S PARTY and get down with Kool & The Gang and their Jungle BOOGIE – https://youtu.be/-BM5wPOe0xQ?is=JcYnM7PLAYnvOEH8 Thank you, Katie, for yet another great challenge! Your puzzles always sing with BRIO. 👏🏻👏🏻

I really really hate that the app info tab doesn’t open to my browser so I can review wordplay as I am solving. It ruins the user experience. It seems to me that the people who created this feature aren’t regular solvers. This needs to be corrected ASAP. 

As to the puzzle, very well done for a Saturday.

For my money, this was a truly magnificent puzzle.

Time after time after time, I'd be rubbing my temples, trying to see something, and I'd realize that the answer I had which sorta fit and was somewhat clever was wrong.  And the right one was better in every way than what I had.

ONION was just brilliant.  I think I had ON__N, and my mind just shoved ONION into the program counter, and suddenly I saw it was a terrific answer to a utterly perplexing clue.

Great moments in this one.

A few learning lookups were required here, to fill in some names I didn’t know. But I hammered away at the rest and eventually got there. Quite a workout, this puzzle.

And I think I’m going to be haunted by images of, um, highly stimulated power saws for quite some time.

Any puzzle with CREAM SODA and RENEE Fleming in it are okay by me.
RENEE, Margaret CHO and WEEB Ewbank were the first entries I felt sure  of filling in.  And then I had HENRY Louis Gates in the SE corner, who made me take out SEAturtle, a bit of a bummer, but thanks HENRY. 
And the clue for RINGO was fun.
In fact, the entire puzzle was a pleasure to solve. 
Thanks for the fun, Katie.

When I dialed the soda company, I got a fizzy signal.

(But I think they have cola ID.)

I’m sure to some extent folks who successfully solve a puzzle rate the puzzle higher than those who weren’t in the constructor’s wheelhouse and the comments skew that way but…

Today was significantly better than yesterday’s puzzle, in my humble opinion.  I ran out of patience running the alphabet on all the naticks yesterday.  Here’s a vote for adding a “Run the Alphabet key” to the app to take some of the slog out of those puzzles.

Today’s puzzle was plenty hard and the design left me work to do in all four corners without much to connect to, but the clues and answers seemed much more polished. There were names I didn’t know today, too, but much more interspersed with clever, thought provoking clues. 

For reference, I’ve solved every puzzle for the past 7 1/2 years so I don’t relate to the good old days of impossible Saturdays, but this was a good puzzle.

Very well done, Katie.  Keep ‘em coming.
Tough and wonderful. I was convinced I was going to have to look things up, and then things clicked. Great clues, and great words -- even the fill.

Took me almost an hour. Twice as long as yesterday. But both gold, thankfully. 
Watching Türkiye embarrass themselves at the World Cup probably doesn’t help solve time. 

This was a nice Saturday challenge. Methinks.  I liked the grid design as well. 

Do you really set a TIMER on the stove?  Or next to it?  and do you really need a timer for cooking?  Baking sure. Cooking on the stove is a pretty active activity for me. Not a set it and forget it. 

Celebratory opening. I couldn’t get around an actual event versus a saying. 

Saw getting turned on. Might be the most brilliant, cringy clue I’ve seen in a long time. 

The long entries were great conversational phrases. 

No Hebrew. Very little proper nouns. I wonder if the brenda howard shout out was out in for Juneteenth. 

Katie hoody got mad skillz.

It's official - Katie Hoody is my favorite constructor.

There are others I look forward to, a handful I always enjoy, but recently, nobody nails it Every Single Time like she does.

"For a minute I wondered if the “Dad” in question was the same everyman behind all the jokes, but it’s surely a reference to the lineup of sodas made by Dad’s Root Beer, a Chicago brand founded in the 1930s."

Now that's elegant.
Such clever, funny clues. Maybe my favorite Saturday ever.

Can anyone tell me what the deal with 45A is?  

I see an I ("eye") or and l ("ell") or a 1, but I can see the connection to EGO.

So, how’d the puzzle go today?
NOT MY FINEST WORK
And how long did it take you to solve it?
LET’S NOT EVEN GO THERE

;-)

Did anyone else assume “child” was the answer to what might lead to a pile of dirty laundry?

I loved most of this puzzle, but really didn’t love some bits. A curate’s egg!

This was a great puzzle, IMNSHO.  Lots of wordplay and answers that were opaque until they weren't. 

Ok a less positive note, it's probably a sign of the times, but I don't like the puzzle being demoted from the top of the app   It's disconcerting and a bit dismaying everytime I open the app.
Whoa, Notmyfinestwork, itsodd but letsnotevengothere.

I thought this was a good puzzle.
It was hard but not because of too many trivias or arcane words but rather by clever clueing.

I saw her name and I hooted. I literally did. 

Startled the cat, even, as she's not used to such εjαcυιατιοηs (settle, Andrzej!) from her human. 

And from this couch, spent, lazing and stupefied, basking in the glow of this golden grid I do declare that if Queen Robyn has well and truly fled: The Queen is abdicated...long live the Queen...Katie Hoody.

Another delightful diabolical clue.

58A [ Natural resource seen in arboretums?] ORE.

Just amazing.

I was hung up with NOTMYFINESTHOUR for a long time. I immediately got the meeting of pop in 1A, and tried ROOTBEER; if it had fit I probably would have been stuck with it for a long time. As it was, it took me quite a while to get it.

From all the love for this puzzle, I'm going to assume it is popular amongst Americans.

For anyone not intimately familiar with American history and culture this was unsolveable. There was no balance between difficult answers and getable ones.

So for now, I will say that this was a terrible puzzle for everyone else. What has happened to the creators that could make a difficult puzzle without resorting to arcane and regional knowledge?

All that being said, I thing the clues were very challenging and unique, and if the creator would close the encyclopedia for a second I think she could create some legendary puzzles!

Another Katie Hoody puzzle, another POY nomination.  

What else is new?

Enjoyed solving this puzzle, even though I needed to use the Check Puzzle a few times. "Don't even go there!" "It was not my finest work". But it's done so "Let's party ". Pour the "cream soda!". "It's odd" , I do prefer Dr Brown's Cream soda (or even better Dr Brown's celery soda), ("Or so I hear)". Have a great weekend!
Elegant puzzle. Mind you I had the auto check on, but in my book that's not cheating, it's learning :D

Can’t believe I broke down on a typo as obvious as IMONTIYOU for IMONTOYOU, leading to TILT (which kinda works) and therefore GREATTAKES (which doesn’t, though I assumed I was just ignorant of some Americanism from the early 80s). A puzzle I really would have liked to have completely solved.

So, let’s start with the clue for RINGO – [Man’s name whose first four letters spell a word describing its last letter].  There I was, trying to think of names starting with “soft” or “long” – already misdirected.  Then I learn post-solve that in the more than 100 times this answer has appeared in Crosslandia, it has never been clued like this.

That’s Katie Hoody and cluing.  She finds new angles.  She looks in Left Field.  

Here’s one more – [Sound made when something snaps into place].  There I was again, searching for words for that satisfying click, misdirected.  When the answer AHA finally came, it came with a “Hah!”  Katie’s cluing is often very funny as well as original, keeping me in a good mood even as my brain is huffing and puffing.  

Katie, you are sterling cluing talent.  This on top of the fresh answers that spark your grids and the remarkable lack of junk answers therein.  Thank you for another splendid outing!
Now I’m craving a Graf’s Swiss Cream from my youth while growing up in Milwaukee.
Some puzzles are worth their extended solve times. This one certainly was.
Very hard puzzle, but still enjoyable!  (took me well over an hour to solve)

Fun Saturday! Now at the point where I see Katie Hoody’s byline on a themeless and start licking my chops — and this didn’t disappoint. Ended up right around my Saturday average which these days counts as a worthy challenge!
SOFT for STAT and TIME for TOLL held me back from conquering the northeast. Great Saturday.

Found this one somewhat easier than yesterday's, but still a solid weekend puzzle. Solved it without assistance in about twenty-five minutes.

Highlight for me was ONION RINGo vertically near the center.

Didn't know BRENDA, RENEE, HONG, SEADRAGON, DORIS or SOL (as clued). Also didn't know the precise locations of CAPRI or Rijeka. (So much trivia!  ;-) )

But all of these were easy to get from the crosses and a bit of commonsense reasoning.

Was slowed down by having GEEKALERT before NERDALERT, and by the lack of Hebrew words in the grid.

Arboretums also made me wince, but just a little.
I said it before and I’ll say it again: more Katie, more Robyn

Woke up with a cough
that makes my headache throb,
and a challenging day ahead.

“But, there *is* a Saturday puzzle…
Gotta check it out…

A Hoody?
For reals?

Did.
Not.
Disappoint.

A little easier than yesterday, but still a good workout. For starts, I knew four of the seven names: RENEE, CHO, WEEB and HENRY, but then I was stuck for quite a long time. I don't fill in answers on my dead tree sheet until I'm sure of them, so that slowed down the process. Another puzzle that I was sure I couldn't finish without help, until I did.

Great friendly diversions: CHUTE, POWER TOOL, BOOGIE, and very little junk fill. A little too many conversational phrases for my taste, but it's Saturday.

One thing that confounds me: Why do we call vanilla soda CREAM SODA? I love it, especially Dr. Brown's, which I haven't had in the decades since I stopped drinking sugary soda and then even diet soda.
Ugh. 37A says it all.

Yes, yes, yes! Thank you, Katie Hoody, for another amazing puzzle!

There was so much to ADORE here: SNOW BIRDS, SEA DRAGON, NERD ALERT. The clues for 26A and 48A were pure genius. I wanted 46D to be cabins, since that's what I stay in when I visit Minnesota's GREAT LAKE.
Tough but fair!
Whooo that one was great! One of those epic puzzles that took me on a roller coaster ride from utter hopelessness to delicious satisfaction. I enjoyed every minute!
Can someone explain CHUTE for dirty laundry? Thanks.

A proper Saturday workout, great column and even better constructor comments.  Always something to learn here.  

Alas, I've been working on yesterday's for over 24 hours (not in a row) but had to throw in the towel - totally stuck in the NW and after seeing the solution don't think another 24 hours would have helped.   

So some welcome toughies to finish up the week.  Many thanks.
Not my finest work... It's oddly satisfying though, because of the combination of obscure proper names and tricky clueing.

Great puzzle to solve ATLEISURE before heading to work. irkED instead of RILED definitely slowed me down in the NW which was the last corner to fall. I got the Gold Star in 21:07 which is my sweet spot for Saturday.

Was out of town for a few days, so just now starting to catch up.
Sunday was a bust because of 3 unknown answers intersecting: CONGEE/UNOS/EGO.
Wednesday was hard.  Took well over my average time, and was just happy to finish.
Thursday was fun and fast -- welcome after Wednesday's puzzle.

Just noticed that after some number of days, I can no longer post or reply to a comment, but I can recommend it.

I got the two spanners more easily than much of the rest. Great, tricky cluing. Made it a challenge to get the names I didn't know through the crosses. Happily I do remember WEEB Eubank from the Jets of my childhood. 
I was running wash, so we won't talk time, but a gold star is a gold star! Happy Saturday!

Katie says she hopes this doesn't turn out to be her finest work, but personally I'd be thrilled if all her future puzzles were merely as great as this one.

But why must we endure the liberal agenda of the editors? First they foist upon us the sacrilegious "theory" that humans descended from apes.

Since that didn't pan out, now they would have us believe that we are descendants of cream soda?! Do better, NYT! Better yet, repent.

Was stuck on SW for a good second when I thought that a "blushing matter" was a ROUSE and not a ROUGE...
Fun puzzle
What a great start to the day! Absolutely first-rate cluing which brought smiles to my face upon figuring them out. Thank you, Katie!
Two nice toughies in a row! Made this one a little harder by leaving my anchor on the deck
Between yesterday and this puzzle, two great ones in a row. Thanks to both constructors.
I loved that i was puzzling over the 5 Great Lakes, realizing that none of them fit

So! Many! Wonderfu! Clues!

My solve began pretty fast, and for about half the puzzle I seriously suspected someone had inadvertently (or advertently) switched the Friday and Saturday puzzles this week. Now a swath around the NW-SE diagonal was filled in. But then I got into the rest of the puzzle and its magnificent clueing struck me like a cosh. I finally solved the entire puzzle but for an  unfortunate DORIc crossing DEcK, since I didn't know the analyst and "anchor DESK" never occurred to me.

The sooner I see more puzzles by this constructor, the sooner I will enjoy her extraordinary clueing.

What gorgeous cluing!  Although I'm a huge fan of tricky, clever Thursday-style themes, sometimes all you need to make a themeless puzzle hugely enjoyable is imaginative, colorful and off-beat clues.  There were so many today:  CREAM SODA; SWING; PLOT POINT; CHUTE; SINGS and the one that gave me by far the most trouble: TOAST.  Wonder if it's new; it's certainly very clever.

Not the hardest Saturday I've ever done, but one of the most entertaining.  I feel that Katie had a lot of fun with the cluing and wanted us to have fun too.

Yikes! Another toughie. Two days in a row going twenty minutes over my average.

My first pass I got WEEB, CHO, and RENEE, and confidently filled in housE/KEYS. 

It was slow overall, but especially the NE. I had gobiG before SWING, and I almost convinced myself that for 12D BOOHOOHOO would work. NOTMYFINESTWORK, even though for a good long time the NEST misled me to filling in hoNEST in the middle of the spanner.

CabinS and CanoeS before CONDOS, tshirtgun before SNOPES put a stop to that.

The challenges and chances for alternate solutions made this a fun one. Thanks!

Cream Soda (usually termed American Cream Soda)  is readily available in UK so I didn't relate to "Dad's pop" - has it gone out of fashion? 
I agree there are many fantastic clues here, (ONION of course, and POWER TOOL) although I didn't know the names. Some were hard for me because of cultural gaps eg why is a CONDO particularly a holiday rental?  and never heard of a laundry CHUTE. 
I don't get why you would call "YEAH"? A password?

I'm glad that our 'senior solvers' approve!
Liked some of the clever clues, but hated the overabundance of proper names of obscure people.

This was a clever construction and a mostly enjoyable solve, but I really do not appreciate these constant random names being thrown in and poorly placed for seemingly no reason. 
The vast majority of these on the Fri & Sat grids are unknown to most solvers. Yesterday was a lot worse than today.
21A, 34A & 56A were gettable w/the surrounding clues which is fine. 43A & 62A were unenjoyable considering the vagueness of 44D, 47A & especially 55D (Deck makes much more sense). I don't see how this set up "challenges" solvers.

Okay.....to everyone who accused The NYT of giving us puzzles that are too easy:  I hope this "Stifles comment, colloquially" (three words, 12 squares, one snarl.)  

This took me at least an hour.  Seven (or more?) names; I knew only three; and WEEB was not one of them...Srsly?  
But you didn't want the NAME of the GREAT LAKE; how is that fair?  

Don't Bring That Up. Not for Discussion.  Not my Finest Hour.  
FIRE ANT.  Not a fond memory.  
Must go lick my wounds now.  It's storming again.

This took me almost twice as long as the average recent Saturday and left me feeling twice as pleased with myself when I solved without lookups. It was fun from start to finish and worth every minute. 

I’d like to talk about proper names, the number of which is often the subject of complaint. Once you have a cross or two, they occupy a spectrum from uninferable to inevitable. Human names tend toward the inevitable, especially when narrowed by gender or some other helpful restriction. WEEB was an exception here, but one could confidently guess BRENDA or Betty/Betsy knowing the B. I never heard of Helen HONG before, but with an H and a G it was difficult to think of alternatives other than Haig. Geography can be much tougher, full of quirky, singular names that can seem uninferable, which is probably why we call a natick a natick and not a smith or a jones. Which leads me to suggest that criticism of proper names should focus more on quality than quantity, dinging only those that don’t give us half a chance if we happen to lack special knowledge in a narrow field.

I'm in a festive mood after yesterday's USMNT victory. LET'S PARTY! And then get down, BOOGIE OOGIE OOGIE. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R-BIJvnNg9I This '70s flashback brought to you by Dad's CREAM SODA.

Well I thought that puzzle was deliciously puzzling and pretty darn fine work - thanks for all the fun, Katie Hoody!

(Anyone else try CHild for "What might lead to a large pile of dirt laundry"??)

i don't know where else i'm supposed to complain about this, but the 5 for 5 Wordle achievement used to say "PLAY Wordle on all 5 days" but was changed to "successfully complete all 5 puzzles" after Friday when it was a fake word (EMOJI). i didn't even think EMOJI was in the Wordle dictionary! is anyone else upset about this? i feel like i was robbed of a one time achievement.

This was hard but fair. Had to call in the cavalry (my wife) and together we got it squared. That SW corner was crispety crunchety. Had everything right except had DECK instead of DESK. I was thinking ship anchor, which was I think the intentional misdirect. DORIC didn't seem entirely impossible as a name so I was stuck flyspecking all over the map. Finally turned it off for a while and the answer literally came to me in a dream as my brain clicked that it had to be DESK.

Thanks for the fun 68 minutes. This was a pretty clever one.
Why does a puzzle have to be symmetrical?

This one is going on my Griddies list! Thoroughly enjoyed it even though I only managed to get 3 right answers before turning on the AutoCheck. I’m not a late-week solver … yet. Loved ONION (7D) and SNOWBIRDS (31D) and so many others. Thank you Katie Hoody for a delightful Saturday puzzle (even if I couldn’t solve it without a lot of help)!
So many people who have never seen a laundry chute! If you can find the right moments, your childhood will be enriched beyond measure by what you can send down one.
Excellent Saturday puzzle. Tough but a totally fair challenge. Took me a bit more time than usual but never felt like a slog. Getting each corner done was a little triumph. Well done!
I really really liked this one. Lots of good head scratchers in there. No junk and A+ cluing. More from Katie please.
Another big hit to my Saturday average.  But a welcome one!
Since when is "solve" a noun? Barf.
Really enjoyed this puzzle! Fantastic cluing! Thank you to the creator for putting this delight together.

"Sound made when something snaps into place?" indeed. 

Just a perfect Saturday in my opinion. Halfway through my first pass I was sure it was impossible, but then it slowly filled in, section by section, with at least half a dozen very satisfying AHA moments.

Enjoyed this puzzle!

Can someone explain why 62 across was clued with the abbreviation “N.B.A.”? I assumed that the answer would also contain an abbreviation. Or is this convention not always followed?

Bill

I have CREAMSODA in my refrigerator now--along with Black Cherry, Root Beer, Lemon-Lime and plain seltzer. But my CREAMSODA is sugar free, sweetened with Stevia. The brand is Zevia. Yum.

Do you know that CREAMSODA is really vanilla-flavored?

Those of us who are too online associate WEEB with the short form of “weeabo,” a somewhat pejorative term for people who are very enthusiastic about anime and manga. I had no idea it was used as a name and was convinced I had the crosses wrong on 43A—I wonder if Mr. Ewing would be entertained to see how his nickname is used on social media these days.

The NYT redeemed themselves today after yesterday's arcane slog. Kudos to the creator as well for putting it together. As others mentioned, this puzzle was challenging for all the right reasons. For me a perfect combination of some difficult trivia, wordplay and potentially useful information that with a reasonable amount of time, patience and maybe a look up or two kept one engaged and felt satisfying upon solving. Thank you again.

I was thrilled to see Katie's name today, and this one did not disappoint! So many fun clues and clever misdirects. Breezy but with plenty of resistance.

I had Helen HuNt until the very end....I mean, maybe she has a podcast, right?! 

This was a perfect example of a sprinkling of "trivia". I don't think I knew a single one of the names, and yet they were all perfectly gettable. 
Come back again soon, Katie!

Last week on a trip back home I took the kids to the deli I grew up going to, and my son wanted to try the Dr. Brown's Cream Soda*. I told him that was my father's favorite. So 1A rattled me! Is cream soda what everyone's dad used to get? Did the constructor tap into my memories? 

Well, apparently "Dad's" is a brand, but I'm going to pretend I don't know that. 

*He loved it, and we had to go back for more the next day.
Even if I struggled a bit, I really enjoyed this puzzle.  It was accessible and didn't feel like someone had just googled obscure items to generate a tough puzzle.
Rarely comment on these, but this was an exceptional puzzle. Very clever clues, high-quality fill, and two impressive and fun spanners. Kudos to the constructor.

I give this puzzle one of the highest compliments I can give:
This was the hardest puzzle I solved (without look-ups) in many years!  And that includes solving in the Archives from 10+ years ago.

Of course, being difficult doesn't necessarily make a puzzle excellent.  This one is because with each difficult answer I puzzled out I didn't say "Ugh!" or "That's not a real word." or "That was an unfair clue."
But rather "How clever!" and "I should've seen that earlier!"

This exemplifies what I like in a puzzle.
But someone tell me what this says about me:  that I enjoy feeling "No way will I be able to solve this", yet somehow I do.
Why is that a wonderful feeling?

Thanks, Kate Hoody!  Great puzzle!
I hope you make us suffer in this enjoyable way again soon!

Almost all of my initial across guesses were correct, unlike yesterday when _none_ of them were. But then I got to 39D, which I was sure had to be sHOo, while the across spanner clearly called for WORK. What final letter could possibly make WHO_ mean to get out? This was a lovely misdirect that took a moment to suss out. Then I started noticing the other misdirects, which were also fun.

I finished today's 10 minutes faster than yesterday's, but after spending way more time than I meant to on the 2007 Bob Klahn puzzle that Jack linked to in his constructor notes, and getting a late start today, having a smooth delightful solve was just the ticket.
As of a couple of hours ago, yesterday's comments section was still pretty active. Almost 600 comments and counting.
WAAAY too many cutesy “question mark” clues for my taste

I loved how one thing led to another.

Some word checks, two lookups. A little sorry I didn’t take more time and finish this later. These puzzles deserve time. 

About the difficult/easy debate(more about yesterday’s): 

Once my mom and I were doing a Friday by accident as we thought it was a Tuesday. We did it in pencil on paper and when we were done I checked the comments.
